headline: Crash recovery under `onPopFailure: 'burn'` no longer destroys an entry no one ever read; the store's clock posture is documented, and the backend contract gains a delivery signal so a custom backend certifies the same guarantee.
summary: The default `'restore'` policy has always kept a failed or crashed read's entry for retry. `'burn'` is the opt-in that destroys a read entry on the assumption that a read attempt may have observed its bytes -- but crash recovery applied it even to a claim whose process died before streaming a single byte, turning a crash before any read into silent data destruction that burn's own rationale does not justify. Recovery is now delivery-gated: it burns an orphaned `'burn'` claim only if a byte actually reached a consumer, and restores one that never delivered. A live `'burn'` read that fails mid-drain is still burned, because it demonstrably delivered bytes. Backends learn a small, persistent delivery signal to make this decision survive the crash, the conformance harness certifies it, and the store's wall-clock posture -- and its behavior under a clock step in either direction -- is now written down.

Fixed: Under `onPopFailure: 'burn'`, crash recovery destroyed a claim even when the crashed process had streamed no byte to any consumer, turning a crash before any read into silent data destruction. Recovery now burns an orphaned burn claim only if a byte actually reached a consumer; one that never delivered is restored, not destroyed (the default `'restore'` policy was always safe). A live burn read that fails mid-drain is still burned -- it demonstrably delivered bytes -- so the opt-in guarantee is unchanged for the case it was designed for.
Changed: The SPEC.md 9 backend contract gains `markDelivered(id, token)` and a `delivered` field on `listClaims()`, and `claim()` now returns an identity `token`: a backend records, persistently, that a byte of a specific claim reached a consumer, so a later process's recovery can tell an observed read from an unobserved one -- and a mark issued for a claim since resolved (an id re-claimed for a budgeted read's next pass) carries the old identity and is ignored. The conformance harness (`@blamejs/stash/conformance`) certifies it, so a custom backend proves the same delivery-gated guarantee against the same cases the in-tree backends pass. `verify()` gains an `orphan-delivered` finding -- a delivery marker whose claim is gone is inert (ids never repeat) but is reaped under repair as layout residue.

The store's clock posture is now documented (SPEC.md 7.2): every time-based decision -- expiry, claim-lease freshness, tombstone pruning -- reads the wall clock, because each must survive a restart and, for expiry and pruning, agree across replicas. Expiry and pruning are deterministic (an absolute `expiresAt` / `destroyedAt`, not skew-sensitive); a live reader's claim is immune to a clock step; and a backward step only defers a destruction the push-time terms already permit -- it never destroys an entry early nor lets one outlive its terms.

The README documents materializing an entry to a file in a single, digest-verified copy (`pipeline(await stash.apply(ref), createWriteStream(dest))`). The store deliberately offers no `materializeTo` method: a store-side copy would give up the digest-verified read, cross the store's write-scope boundary, and work only on a filesystem backend, where piping the verified stream is already single-copy and backend-agnostic.
